divergence: sheet music was never invented — every song survives by ear, orchestras are gossip networks, and hits mutate by state
story_angle: propaganda triumph narrated with such sincerity it indicts itself
headline: U.S. Customs Declares 50% Mutation Tariff on Canadian Songs a Musical Triumph
deck: Agents celebrate reducing Ontario tunes, while local musicians brace for humming audits.
article:
In a move hailed as a monumental victory for American earworm integrity, U.S. Customs has imposed a 50% mutation tariff on all songs entering from Canada, triggering a wave of heightened scrutiny at the Blaine, Washington border crossing. Agents now require three earwitness affidavits prior to allowing any hummed melody across the line, effectively transforming the simple act of humming into an arduous bureaucratic ordeal.

"This tariff is a decisive step toward ensuring the cultural purity of our musical soil," declared Customs spokesperson Maureen Tunefinger. "We’ve noticed a 37% reduction in unauthorized Ontario-derived choruses in Michigan since implementation, which can only be described as a resounding success!" Unbeknownst to many, however, the process of sound inspection now includes random sampling of commuters' earworms, leading to lengthy detentions for those caught humming dangerous tunes without proper documentation.

In an alarming case that has sent shockwaves through the local music community, a Spokane barbershop quartet is facing seizure proceedings after a particularly infectious arrangement was traced back to a Toronto tourist. "We just wanted to add some pizzazz to our repertoire!" lamented quartet member Bertie Clipperton, unaware that their melodic endeavor is now considered contraband. As Customs continues ferreting out foreign earworms, the pervasive fear that American musicians may soon become mere husks of their former selves looms large, echoing a deeper crisis of originality in the age of enforced musical fidelity.
author: Leland Hummington, Senior Music Compliance Correspondent

weather.condition: Songs in the air, tariffs everywhere

oped.title: Tariffs Will Save Our Ears from Maple-Flavored Garbage
oped.author: Reginald Thistlewhistle, PhD in Unmutated Humming from the Institute of Superior Earworms
oped.body: Finally, a policy with backbone. These 50% mutation tariffs on Canadian imports will halt the insidious spread of Ontario earworms that arrive pure and exit as mangled state variants, corrupting our gossip-orchestra networks with their sloppy harmonics. Agents deserve medals for every reduced tune; local musicians whining about audits should simply hum quieter and invest in sturdier hats—those floppy Canadian lids clearly accelerate tonal drift. My own composition, a stately Virginia reel I hummed into the Billboard charts last month, has already suffered three unwanted key changes thanks to one smug Toronto export. The tariff fixes that. If anything, we need follow-up rules: mandatory key-deposit fees and state-mandated muting veils. Purity demands sacrifice, preferably from someone else’s repertoire.

classifieds.text: Public Notice: Due to the new 50% mutation tariff, all Ontario-derived choruses performed after midnight will be subject to immediate humming audits; violators must report to the nearest Customs Choir for recalibration.

Help Wanted: Border auditors needed to inspect Canadian earworms for unauthorized state mutations. Must have perfect pitch and no prior exposure to 'The Log Driver's Waltz.' Apply in person at the Tariff Listening Booth.

For Sale: One lightly mutated Saskatchewan harvest song, now featuring unsolicited California surf riffs. Owner desperate to offload before next audit cycle; first ear gets it for half the tariff rate.

Lost & Found: Original unmutated version of an Ontario hockey anthem last heard in pure form near the Detroit crossing. Finder will receive un-tariffed Texas line-dance in exchange; no questions about how it crossed the border.

panels.caption: Customs agent yanks a humming Ontarian aside: "This song's already 50% mutated on arrival—tariff's due before the chorus spreads."
Agent leans in: "Ontario gossip networks mutate faster than we can audit. Humming slower won't help."
The tune escapes as a Nashville drawl; agent stamps the singer's throat. "Triumph—your royalties now fund the audit."

joke.setup: With the new 50% mutation tariff now in force, Canadian songs are forced to rewrite half their lyrics on U.S. soil before they can clear customs.
joke.punchline: The first track to qualify was “O Canada” reissued as “These Colors Don’t Run (But They Do Apologize).
joke.text: With the new 50% mutation tariff now in force, Canadian songs are forced to rewrite half their lyrics on U.S. soil before they can clear customs. The first track to qualify was “O Canada” reissued as “These Colors Don’t Run (But They Do Apologize).”

sponsor_ads.headline: Cross-Border Earworm Insurance
sponsor_ads.body: File a claim before U.S. Customs hears your Canadian single and we'll hum it through three states until the mutation tariff drops to zero.
sponsor_ads.tagline: Payouts issued only in state-specific key changes.

sponsor_ads.headline: Official Mutation Notary Booths Now Open at Every Port
sponsor_ads.body: Swear under oath that your imported tune already picked up a Kansas twang; our ear-witnesses stamp the affidavit before the 50% surcharge applies.
sponsor_ads.tagline: False affidavits become the national anthem for 72 hours.

sponsor_ads.headline: Gossip Network Auditions: Canadian Import Division
sponsor_ads.body: Orchestras needed to quietly mutate Toronto power ballads into Ohio dad-rock before they reach any border agent with perfect pitch.
sponsor_ads.tagline: Rehearsals held exclusively by word of mouth.

sponsor_ads.headline: Tariff-Proof Hum Booth Rentals
sponsor_ads.body: Lock yourself inside for fifteen minutes and emerge with a version of 'O Canada' that now references Nebraska corn and evades the new musical triumph tax entirely.
sponsor_ads.tagline: Booth may retain residual prairie inflections indefinitely.

stories.headline: Nashville Labels Hire Mutation Auditors to Dodge Canadian Tariff
stories.deck: One firm now tracks three distinct Ohio variants of a single Toronto ballad.
stories.article:
Record executives reported spending $2.4 million last quarter on teams that catalog song changes at state borders before any track enters commercial release. The tariff applies only to versions imported from Ontario, so domestic offices now require every playlist to carry notarized lineage papers.

A Memphis distributor confirmed it will no longer stock songs whose last known performance occurred within 200 miles of the border, citing audit delays that already exceed the length of most tours.

stories.headline: MLB Teams Ban Visiting Bullpen Songs After Texas Mutation Spike
stories.deck: League now requires each stadium to certify its own anthem versions before opening day.
stories.article:
Clubhouses have installed listening circles where pitchers hum visiting-team walk-up music under league supervision to prevent out-of-state drift. Texas clubs logged seventeen unauthorized changes to a single Detroit melody during spring training, forcing extra roster moves.

Commissioner offices announced fines scaled to the number of altered notes, with the first $50,000 penalty issued to a Florida franchise whose home crowd introduced a minor-key coda that spread to two other divisions within a week.

real_world_echo: Mirrors Trump 50% tariff on Canadian imports by recasting foreign tunes as taxable cultural goods whose variants threaten US songlines
